repo.name: ky
repo.description: 🌳 Tiny & elegant JavaScript HTTP client based on the Fetch API

level.name: Assisted
level.description: AI tools are adopted by many developers and basic guardrails exist (some tests, a linter, CI runs). There are no shared AI conventions, so benefits are individual rather than systemic.

posture.label: Getting Started
posture.blurb: Early on both AI adoption and engineering rigor — pick one axis to advance first.

dimensions.name: AI Tooling & Conventions
dimensions.summary: No machine-readable AI or agent guidance of any kind is present. There are no CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, .cursorrules, or copilot-instructions.md files, no MCP configuration, no .claude/ directory, and no prompt library. For a solo maintainer of a widely-used library this is unsurprising, but it means every AI session must rediscover build commands, test patterns, architectural constraints, and conventions from scratch — context is neither shared nor persistent across contributors or tools.
evidence: No machine-readable AI/agent guidance detected (e.g. CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, .cursorrules)
strengths: Well-structured TypeScript source with clear module boundaries gives AI tools an inferrable architectural scaffold
Inline JSDoc on all public error classes is precise enough to partially substitute for explicit agent orientation
gaps: No CLAUDE.md, AGENTS.md, or equivalent file documenting build/test/run commands for agent consumption
No architectural constraints or 'never/always' rules an AI tool could follow to stay in bounds
No MCP configuration or tool-permission policy
No prompt library or slash-command conventions for common maintenance tasks

dimensions.name: Automated Testing
dimensions.summary: The test suite is a genuine standout. AVA drives 23 test files at a 0.79 test-to-source ratio with concurrent execution, and Playwright runs real cross-browser end-to-end tests against Chromium, Firefox, and WebKit — a rare rigor signal for a library. Sampled tests demonstrate thorough behavioral and edge-case discipline: test/body-size.ts exercises every TypedArray variant including subarray slicing and multi-byte Unicode; test/base-url.ts covers 18+ URL-resolution corner cases against a live Express server; test/browser.ts wires Playwright against a live ESM server. Codecov integration tracks coverage trends. The suite stops just short of 95 because no mutation testing (Stryker), API-contract testing, or coverage-threshold gates in CI are present.
evidence: Sampled tests assert behavior (60 substantive assertions across 4 sampled test file(s))
strengths: Multi-browser e2e via Playwright (Chromium, Firefox, WebKit) against a live ESM server is exceptionally rare in library projects
0.79 test-to-source ratio with 23 files indicates disciplined test-alongside-source culture across the codebase
Edge-case rigor is high: TypedArray subarray slicing, multi-byte Unicode, 18+ URL-resolution variants, and live HTTP server fixtures all covered
Codecov badge confirms coverage reporting is integrated and publicly tracked
gaps: No mutation testing (Stryker) to verify that the suite actually catches injected faults
No coverage-threshold enforcement in CI — coverage gaps could silently widen across PRs
No contract or type-level tests for the public TypeScript API surface beyond expect-type spot checks
No performance or throughput smoke tests to catch request-pipeline regressions

dimensions.name: CI/CD & Delivery
dimensions.summary: A GitHub Actions workflow runs lint (xo), build (tsc), and AVA/Playwright tests across a Node.js 22/24/latest matrix on every push and pull_request — solid baseline CI for a library. However, the delivery pipeline ends there. Branch protection is entirely absent (confirmed by process signals: requires PR: no, required approvals: 0, status checks: none), meaning CI results are advisory rather than gates and a broken commit can be merged silently. Releases are driven by the manual `np` CLI, not by CI automation. There is no release-please, semantic-release, or changesets integration; no npm cache in the setup-node step; and no separate pipeline stages for lint, build, and test.
strengths: CI triggers on both push and pull_request providing broad event coverage
Node.js 22/24/latest matrix with fail-fast: false catches version-specific regressions in parallel
Playwright browser installation in CI enables genuine cross-browser validation on every run
Single `npm test` integrates lint, build, and test in a deterministic order (lint fails fast)
gaps: Branch protection is entirely absent — CI results cannot gate merges on main
No release automation in CI; releases depend on the maintainer running `np` manually
No npm dependency caching in the workflow, adding unnecessary latency to every CI run
Single serial job with no parallelism between lint, build, and test stages

dimensions.name: Agentic Workflows
dimensions.summary: No agentic or AI-in-CI workflows exist. There are no AI review bots (CodeRabbit, Greptile, claude-code-action), no LLM steps in any CI job, no auto-fix or auto-PR tooling, and no Dependabot or Renovate configuration for automated dependency updates. Process signals confirm 0% AI involvement across all 40 analyzed pull requests. The repository is entirely human-driven with no autonomous loops of any kind.
evidence: No agentic/AI-in-CI workflows detected (e.g. AI review bots, LLM steps in CI, auto-merge)
gaps: No AI review bot or LLM-in-CI step to close the 59% unreviewed-PR gap
No Dependabot or Renovate for automated dependency update PRs
No auto-fix or auto-format bot for routine maintenance tasks
No issue-to-PR automation

dimensions.name: Documentation & Knowledge
dimensions.summary: The README is substantial at 14,000 characters across 11 sections, covering installation, API reference, hook lifecycle, retry semantics, TypeScript generics, streaming, and browser compatibility — well above average for an open-source library and genuinely consumable by an AI agent seeking context. A secondary signal is the dense JSDoc on all public error classes: HTTPError.ts documents API contracts, pre-parsing behavior, body-size limits, and hook interaction order; KyError.ts explains cross-realm compatibility and type-guard semantics; ForceRetryError.ts documents runtime protection against non-Error causes. However, no CONTRIBUTING.md, CHANGELOG.md, /docs directory, ADRs, or formal architecture document exists.
strengths: README covers installation, full API reference, hooks lifecycle, retry/timeout semantics, and TypeScript generics with concrete examples
Error class JSDoc is dense and precise, explaining cross-realm behavior, body-size limits, and hook ordering — agent-consumable context
Codecov badge provides a visible, persistent coverage signal for contributors assessing test health
README includes migration notes and plain-fetch comparison aiding both newcomers and AI tools assessing scope
gaps: No CONTRIBUTING.md to guide new contributors or AI agents attempting their first change
No CHANGELOG.md — release history requires reading git tags or npm, not a project-managed file
No architecture document explaining module boundaries, design decisions, or ESM-only rationale
No ADRs capturing why key decisions (AVA over Jest, XO over raw ESLint, zero-dependency stance) were made

dimensions.name: Code Quality & Guardrails
dimensions.summary: Code quality tooling is present and coherent: XO (an opinionated ESLint + Prettier wrapper) enforces consistent style with TypeScript-aware rules, and the TypeScript compiler is configured for both distribution and test scenarios. The xo config in package.json shows intentional rule tuning — several overly strict rules are disabled for pragmatic reasons, reflecting considered rather than default configuration. Lint runs before tests in `npm test`, creating an early-fail signal. However, there are no pre-commit hooks, no CODEOWNERS, no commitlint or conventional-commit conventions, and PR review coverage sits at only 41% with zero required approvals — guardrails exist but are not enforced at the merge boundary.
evidence: PR review coverage 41% (29 merged · 68% small · 0% reverted)
strengths: XO integrates ESLint and Prettier into a single tool with TypeScript-aware rules, reducing configuration surface area
TypeScript configured for both distribution (tsconfig.dist.json) and test compilation
Rule configuration is thoughtfully tuned with explicit rationale rather than a stock preset
Linting is placed before tests in the test script, ensuring style gates fail fast
gaps: No pre-commit hooks (husky, lint-staged) — lint and type errors can reach CI without local enforcement
No CODEOWNERS file for automatic reviewer assignment on changed paths
Commit messages do not follow conventional-commit format, blocking changelog and release-note automation
41% PR review rate with zero required approvals means most merged code bypasses both human and automated review

dimensions.name: Commit & Velocity Signals
dimensions.summary: The repository shows an active commit cadence with 30 recent commits and rapid v2.x bug-fix iterations (2.0.0 → 2.0.1 → 2.0.2). However, zero AI co-author trailers appear in any analyzed commit, 0% of the 40 analyzed pull requests involved AI, and commit messages follow no conventional-commit prefix scheme ('Tweaks', 'Meta tweaks', 'Fix CI'). The velocity evidence reflects healthy solo-maintainer cadence, not an AI-augmented workflow. The 68% small-PR rate is a positive structural signal — small, focused changes are easier for AI tools to reason about — but there is no evidence this reflects deliberate AI-ready batching.
strengths: Active commit cadence and rapid bug-fix releases demonstrate healthy project momentum
68% small-PR rate (≤200 line changes) creates a change-batch size well-suited for AI assistance
Rapid 2.0.0→2.0.2 release cycle shows responsive, low-latency maintenance rhythm
gaps: Zero AI co-author trailers in any analyzed commit confirms no AI tooling is in the contribution workflow
No conventional-commit prefixes (fix:, feat:, chore:) limiting any changelog or release-note automation
Informal messages ('Tweaks', 'Meta tweaks') provide minimal semantic signal for automated tooling or bisect workflows
0% AI-involved pull requests across 40 analyzed confirms AI is not part of the contribution process

dimensions.name: AI Process & Harness
dimensions.summary: There is no evidence of a structured AI process or harness anywhere in the repository. No evals, no golden tests for AI output, no structured prompt or agent library, no agent-readable runbooks or ADRs, and no formal contribution workflow (no PR template, no issue template, no Definition-of-Done). The contribution process is entirely informal and tacit. For a solo maintainer this is understandable, but it means AI assistance cannot be systematically leveraged, verified, or reproduced across sessions or contributors.
evidence: No dedicated AI process/harness detected (e.g. evals, prompt library, agent runbooks)
gaps: No evals or golden tests to verify AI-generated or AI-assisted output against expected behavior
No structured prompt or agent library for common maintenance tasks (bug triage, release notes, test generation)
No agent-readable runbooks covering how to safely add a feature, fix a bug, or update a dependency
No PR or issue templates to structure AI-assisted contributions toward mergeable outcomes

dimensions.name: Supply Chain & Security
dimensions.summary: A SECURITY.md policy establishes a responsible disclosure channel — a baseline positive signal. Beyond that, the supply-chain security posture is minimal: no SAST (CodeQL, Semgrep) appears in the CI workflow, no automated dependency vulnerability scanning (Dependabot security alerts, Snyk, OSV-Scanner) is configured, no secret scanning pipeline exists, and no SBOM generation or artifact signing is present. The library's zero-runtime-dependency stance eliminates transitive supply-chain risk for consumers, which is a meaningful structural advantage. Dev dependencies (ava, playwright, express, typescript — all in package.json) remain unscanned.
strengths: SECURITY.md establishes a responsible disclosure policy and reporting contact
Zero runtime dependencies eliminates an entire class of transitive supply-chain risk for all consumers of the library
npm publish via `np` includes pre-publish checks that provide a light integrity gate at release time
gaps: No SAST pipeline (CodeQL, Semgrep) in GitHub Actions to catch code-level vulnerabilities
No automated dependency vulnerability scanning for dev dependencies (Dependabot security alerts, OSV-Scanner)
No secret scanning (gitleaks, trufflehog) in CI to prevent credential leaks in commits
No SBOM generation or artifact signing/provenance for npm releases

headline: ky is a technically excellent library with an outstanding test suite and coherent CI, but sits firmly at L2 (Assisted) — AI tooling, agentic workflows, and AI process infrastructure are entirely absent, so any AI assistance is purely individual, ephemeral, and unverifiable.
strengths: Exceptional automated test suite: 23 test files, 0.79 test-to-source ratio, real cross-browser Playwright e2e, and rigorous edge-case coverage give AI tools a high-confidence safety net for proposed changes
Coherent TypeScript and XO linting create a well-typed, style-enforced codebase that AI tools can navigate and generate correct idiomatic code for
Active small-batch commit cadence (68% small PRs, rapid release iterations) creates a low-merge-debt rhythm that AI assistance slots into naturally
Zero runtime dependencies structurally eliminates transitive supply-chain risk for consumers and keeps the auditable surface area minimal
Detailed README and dense error-class JSDoc provide AI-consumable context about library behavior, API contracts, and hook semantics
risks: Branch protection is entirely absent — CI results are advisory, meaning a broken AI-generated PR could be merged silently into a library with 16k+ stars and widespread downstream consumers

Complete absence of AI tooling conventions (no CLAUDE.md, no contributing guide, no agent runbook) means every AI session starts cold with no shared guardrails, producing inconsistent and unverifiable contributions
No automated dependency or SAST scanning leaves the CI environment and release pipeline unmonitored for vulnerabilities introduced through dev dependencies or code patterns
41% PR review rate with zero required approvals creates a review gap that widens risk as AI-assisted contributions grow — most code merges without human or automated scrutiny
No CONTRIBUTING.md or architecture document means contributors — human or AI — must reverse-engineer conventions, increasing the rate of PRs that require extensive revision before they are mergeable

roadmap.title: Agent guidance is absent — AI tools have no shared scaffold to orient from
roadmap.rationale: Without a CLAUDE.md or equivalent, every AI session must rediscover build commands, test patterns, module conventions, and invariants from scratch. A single well-written orientation file transforms AI assistance from ad-hoc guessing into reliable, repeatable help — especially valuable when external contributors use AI tools to propose changes to a security-sensitive library.
explore: What would a first-time contributor (or an AI tool acting as one) most need to know to make a correct change to ky — which files to touch, which tests to run, what invariants must never be broken?
Are there implicit conventions in the test structure or source layout that AI tools currently misread, which could be made explicit in a short guidance file?
What commands, constraints, or architectural boundaries would you want an AI agent to know before it proposes a change — and which would you want it to ask about rather than assume?

roadmap.title: Branch protection is absent — CI results don't gate merges on main
roadmap.rationale: The outstanding test suite provides confidence during development but not at the merge boundary. For a library consumed by thousands of projects, a broken release is high-blast. Branch protection with required passing status checks is the missing link between 'we have tests' and 'we are protected at merge time' — a prerequisite for trusting any autonomous agent to open PRs without mandatory human review of every one.
explore: What would need to be true for you to feel comfortable with an AI-assisted or community PR merging without you manually verifying CI passed?
Have there been cases where a fix was merged and a test failure was only caught after the fact — what did that recovery look like?
Would requiring a passing CI run before merge change the experience for external contributors in ways you'd want to avoid, or is the friction acceptable?

roadmap.title: Contributing conventions are undocumented — humans and AI agents must infer the rules
roadmap.rationale: No CONTRIBUTING.md, PR template, or Definition-of-Done means the norms for 'what makes a good change to ky' live entirely in the maintainer's head and are re-communicated in review comments. As AI-assisted contribution grows, written conventions dramatically improve the signal-to-noise ratio of incoming PRs and give AI agents a repeatable harness for producing changes worth reviewing rather than requiring extensive back-and-forth.
explore: What do most rejected or heavily revised PRs have in common — is there a short checklist you wish contributors had followed that you find yourself repeating?
If an AI agent were going to open ten PRs on ky, what guidance would you want it to follow to maximize the chance each one was mergeable on the first pass?
Are there test patterns, API design principles, or TypeScript conventions that are currently implicit but that you consistently enforce during code review?

roadmap.title: Dependency and security scanning are manual — supply-chain gaps accumulate silently
roadmap.rationale: Even a zero-runtime-dependency library has a substantial dev dependency surface (ava, playwright, express, typescript, busboy) that is currently unscanned. Automated scanning surfaces vulnerabilities at PR time rather than post-release and is especially important as the project becomes a target for AI-assisted automated updates, where unreviewed dependency bumps could introduce risks without human scrutiny.
explore: How would you currently learn that a dev dependency introduced a vulnerability affecting the CI environment or a contributor's local machine?
Would automated dependency security PRs (Dependabot alerts) feel like useful signal or noise given the current PR volume and review capacity?
Are there security controls you'd want confidently in place before enabling any form of automated dependency update — and what would 'confidently in place' look like to you?

roadmap.title: No agentic review in the contribution loop — the 59% unreviewed-PR gap is unclosed
roadmap.rationale: With only 41% of merged PRs receiving a human review, a large fraction of changes merge without any quality gate beyond CI passing. An AI review bot could close that gap by flagging edge-case misses, TypeScript contract violations, or missing tests automatically — making AI contribution safer and raising the overall bar without requiring more of the maintainer's time.
explore: What kinds of review observations do you find yourself making repeatedly on PRs that a bot could surface automatically — correctness issues, missing test cases, style inconsistencies?
Would an AI reviewer that commented on test coverage and TypeScript correctness feel helpful or disruptive given the current pace of contributions?
If an AI agent opened a PR with a proposed bug fix, what would a minimal automated review gate look like that would give you enough confidence to merge it with light manual inspection?

discrepancies.claim: The D5 signalScore of 30 understates documentation quality: sampled source files (HTTPError.ts, KyError.ts, NetworkError.ts, ForceRetryError.ts) contain dense, multi-paragraph JSDoc covering API contracts, body-size limits, hook ordering, cross-realm type-guard semantics, and runtime protection details — inline documentation richness that the signal did not capture and that a score of 30 fails to reflect.

The D3 signalScore of 50 is likely generous: process signals confirm branch protection is entirely absent (requires PR: no, required approvals: 0, status checks: no), which means the CI pipeline cannot gate merges and its protective value is purely advisory — a structural gap that materially undermines a score of 50 for delivery maturity.
